intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

body.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

draft.title: recipe app for people who hate cooking
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
recipe app for people who hate cooking

Building your first draft…

data.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

facade.hypothesis: People who hate cooking want to see effort required (time/steps) before reading anything else
facade.axisTargeted: Dense data (effort metric) vs narrative (story-driven recipe)
facade.label: Recipe in one number
facade.acceptImplies: Lead with effort signals: cook time, step count, or complexity score. Numbers first, story second.
facade.rejectImplies: Bury the effort cost in text. Lead with narrative, ingredient story, or chef background instead.

evidence.hypothesis: People who hate cooking want to see effort required (time/steps) before reading anything else
evidence.implication: Lead with effort signals: cook time, step count, or complexity score. Numbers first, story second.

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking prefer low-effort, relaxing interfaces—dark mode reduces cognitive load and feels less demanding than bright, busy screens
facade.label: Dark Mode
facade.acceptImplies: Explore moody, high-contrast, low-light design systems; consider nighttime-friendly palettes and calming color schemes
facade.rejectImplies: Pivot toward bright, energetic, high-contrast light interfaces; test whether users want visual stimulation and clarity over calm

facade.hypothesis: For a lazy cook, showing one recipe at full-screen size with everything needed below the fold reduces decision paralysis and scrolling fatigue
facade.label: One giant recipe card
facade.acceptImplies: User values immersion in single recipe over browsing multiple options; build full-screen recipe view as primary pattern
facade.rejectImplies: User wants to see multiple recipes at once or navigate quickly between options; prioritize grid or list layout instead

facade.hypothesis: A recipe app for people who hate cooking should adopt a playful, irreverent voice that validates frustration rather than judging the user's reluctance
facade.axisTargeted: Voice personality — from serious/instructional to playful/comedic
facade.label: Snarky tone
facade.acceptImplies: User wants personality that feels like a friend commiserating, uses humor about cooking difficulty, maybe light sarcasm or casual language
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers neutral, professional, matter-of-fact tone that stays out of the way and focuses purely on functionality without commentary

facade.hypothesis: Fluid, sequential reveals of ingredients (rather than instant full list) reduce cognitive load and create a sense of discovery, making cooking feel less overwhelming for people who hate cooking
facade.axisTargeted: Motion: animated reveal vs instant display
facade.label: Ingredients slide in
facade.acceptImplies: User values gradual, choreographed information unfolding—prefers motion that guides attention and paces content
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers immediate access to all information at once—dislikes animation that delays seeing what they need, even if it's decorative

axes.label: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
Whether user needs heavy filtering (dietary, time, skill) upfront vs. minimal friction and discovery-based browsing
axes.poleA: Lots of filters or keep it simple

axes.label: Whether user wants smooth, polished animations that feel premium vs. snappy, instant feedback that feels responsive and low-friction

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user needs heavy filtering (dietary, time, skill) upfront vs. minimal friction and discovery-based browsing
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants smooth, polished animations that feel premium vs. snappy, instant feedback that feels responsive and low-friction
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users building a 'hate cooking' app may gravitate toward cluttered, authentic, chaotic visual language rather than minimal/sterile interfaces—reflecting the actual cooking experience they're trying to escape or reframe.
facade.axisTargeted: Organic vs Digital — specifically: does the mood lean *lived-in/imperfect* or *polished/designed*?
facade.label: Messy countertop
facade.acceptImplies: The brand should feel tactile, forgiving, humor-forward. Messy is relatable. Think flour dust, random notes, things in the wrong spot. Visual language: asymmetry, hand-drawn, color chaos, real photography.
facade.rejectImplies: Users want the app to be a RETREAT from messy reality—clean, ordered, calm, minimal. The app is the antidote to kitchen stress. Visual language: whitespace, sans-serif, predictability, control.

facade.hypothesis: People who hate cooking want to bail out fast if a recipe looks too hard, so a visible escape route reduces friction and anxiety
facade.axisTargeted: Information Architecture — decision speed vs commitment depth
facade.label: Skip button prominent
facade.acceptImplies: Surface abort mechanisms early; design for quick rejection paths; assume low patience threshold
facade.rejectImplies: People who hate cooking will engage deeply with recipe content if it's well-presented; friction from hard-to-exit flows is not a core pain point

draft.title: QuickBite – Recipe App for People Who Hate Cooking
draft.summary: A recipe app that strips away complexity and delivers dead-simple meals in under 15 minutes with minimal ingredients and zero fuss.
draft.html:
QuickBite

Meals so simple, you'll actually cook them

Filter by mood

Today's picks

Garlic butter pasta

8 min4 ingredients

Boil pasta. Melt butter with garlic. Toss. Done. Seriously.

Avocado toast

3 min3 ingredients

Toast bread. Smash avocado. Add salt. Eat standing up.

Egg fried rice

10 min5 ingredients

Scramble eggs. Toss with rice and soy sauce. Microwave if lazy.

Out of ideas?

Get a random recipe that takes less time than scrolling TikTok
